With the failure of The Wacky World of Tex Avery which met by poor ratings and reviews, it being an "insult" to Tex Avery's legacy, DiC has thinking they might to take the cast of the The Wacky World of Tex Avery and spin off to shows like Pompeii Pete, Einstone, Genghis & Khannie and Power Pooch. 


Les Studios Tex SARL is stii making shows in the works like "Sonic Underground", Sherlock Holmes in the 22nd Century, Archie's Weird Mysteries	and "the untitled stargate movie" is in development.


With the adult animaion is still rising, DiC will try to making an adult only cartoons and still making cartoons of kids and teens.


Meanwhile, Fox Kids's ratings since the 1996-1997 season do great to okay in the ratings, while Kids Wb beting Fox kids in the curve, and Disney's one Saturday morning is beating Fox Kids too, with the 1997-1998 fall season at the time of Saturday-morning and weekdays for kids and teens.

Title: DiC Entertainment Explores Adult Animation as 'South Park' Success Shifts Landscape

Date: October 12, 1997

Los Angeles - In a bold move signaling a change in the industry, DiC Entertainment is reportedly contemplating the creation of animated content tailored for adult audiences, following the unexpected success of Comedy Central's 'South Park.' This marks a pivotal moment as Saturday morning and weekday cartoons have long been the domain of children and teens, with networks such as Cartoon Network and Nickelodeon leading the charge. However, it appears that adults still harbor an appetite for animated escapades, albeit with a more mature twist.

The inspiration to pivot towards adult animation comes on the heels of the dismal performance of 'The Wacky World of Tex Avery.' The show, aimed at a younger audience, has been met with poor ratings and critical reviews, even being called an insult to the legendary animator's legacy. This turn of events has prompted DiC to consider reimagining characters from the 'Wacky World' series into adult-oriented spin-offs, featuring personalities such as Pompeii Pete, Einstone, Genghis & Khannie, and Power Pooch, in an attempt to capture a fraction of the adult demographic.

On the production front, Les Studios Tex SARL continues to forge ahead with projects aimed at younger viewers, including 'Sonic Underground,' 'Sherlock Holmes in the 22nd Century,' 'Archie's Weird Mysteries,' and an 'untitled Stargate movie' currently in development. These shows, coupled with DiC's new strategy, could usher in a diverse portfolio capable of catering to viewers of all ages.

As the trend for adult animation rises, DiC's willingness to produce content exclusively for adults while still serving their traditional child and teen audience could be a calculated risk. The move appears to be a response to the shifting dynamics of viewership patterns and audience demands, with series like 'The Simpsons' and 'South Park' proving that adult humor in animation has a significant and engaged audience.

Meanwhile, the battle for Saturday morning supremacy is in full swing. Fox Kids has reported ratings fluctuating from great to adequate since the 1996-1997 season, while Kids' WB has surged ahead of Fox Kids, and Disney's 'One Saturday Morning' block is also proving to be a formidable competitor. This competitive landscape only emphasizes the need for innovation among networks and content creators.

Should DiC successfully expand into the adult animation sphere, it could mark a new chapter in the company's history and significantly alter the animation market. As for now, all eyes are on DiC Entertainment as industry insiders and fans alike await their next move.

could you write an October 16, 1997 news article about 

 Time Warner purchased Turner Broadcasting System since last year.

The End of Evangelion premied in japan in July 19, 1997 with ¥2.47 billion ($19 million).

 Beavis and Butt-Head will end on November 28 at MTV.

Frederator Incorporated will to be making  Oh Yeah! Cartoons to launch at nick in summer 1998 by Fred Seibert.

Star Wars's Special Edition films being an test run of an new star wars in 1999.

Title: Time Warner-Turner Merger - A Retrospective; 'The End of Evangelion' takes Japan by storm; MTV's 'Beavis and Butt-Head' to Conclude; Frederator Gears Up for 'Oh Yeah! Cartoons'; 'Star Wars' Gears Up for a New Era

Date: October 16, 1997

In the ever-evolving landscape of media and entertainment, this past year has seen significant shifts. Time Warner's acquisition of Turner Broadcasting System in 1996 continues to send ripples through the industry as the conglomerate harnesses the power of a combined content library and distribution network in a move that has redefined the media playing field.

Across the Pacific, 'The End of Evangelion,' which premiered in Japan on July 19, 1997, has taken the anime world by storm, raking in a staggering ¥2.47 billion (approximately $19 million). The film, serving as an alternate ending to the cult classic TV series 'Neon Genesis Evangelion,' has attracted both praise and analysis for its complex narrative and visceral visuals.

On the home front, news has broken that the irreverent animated series 'Beavis and Butt-Head' will air its final episode on MTV on November 28. Since its inception, the show has experienced a tumultuous love-hate relationship with audiences and critics, yet undeniably it has stamped its place in the annals of pop culture history.

In anticipation of the summer of 1998, Fred Seibert's Frederator Incorporated announced 'Oh Yeah! Cartoons,' slated to launch on Nickelodeon. The series, which will feature a collection of original animated shorts, aims to be a breeding ground for the next generation of animation talent and is expected to follow in the footsteps of Seibert's previous successes at the network.

Meanwhile, in the realm of sci-fi cinema, fans are abuzz with the theatrical release of the Special Editions of the 'Star Wars' trilogy. Touted as not merely a nostalgic re-release but also as a test run for the technological and storytelling advancements anticipated in the planned 'Star Wars' prequels set for 1999, George Lucas's space saga looks set to recapture and expand its legendary status among legions of fans both old and new.

As the year progresses, these developments paint a picture of an industry at the crossroads of innovation and nostalgia. With corporate consolidations, groundbreaking international cinema, iconic show conclusions, exciting new ventures in animation, and reinvention of classic film franchises, the stage is set for a dynamic and transformative entertainment era.

a Final fantasy 7 review

Title: "Anastasia" Twirls into Theaters; "Beavis and Butt-Head" Say Farewell; Toonami Gears Up for "Robotech"; Reviews for "Johnny Bravo" and "Cow and Chicken"; "The Wacky World of Tex Avery" Canceled; "Banjo-Kazooie" Delayed; "Final Fantasy VII" Shines

Date: November 14, 1997

On the cinematic frontier, the enchanting world of animation receives a regal touch as Don Bluth and Gary Goldman's "Anastasia" premieres to audiences nationwide. This feature marks Bluth's first acclaimed hit since "All Dogs Go to Heaven" in 1989, garnering attention as the standout offering from Fox Animation Studios. Venturing into the realm of historically inspired storytelling, "Anastasia" is poised to capture the hearts of viewers with its lush animation, compelling narrative, and a memorable soundtrack, arguably placing it atop the Fox Animation roster.

In television news, MTV's "Beavis and Butt-Head" are set to grace screens for their final act on November 28. The notoriously slack-minded duo has entertained and provoked since their inception, and their departure is sure to leave a void in the hearts of fans who reveled in their irreverent humor.

Cartoon Network's Toonami block continues to ride the wave of nostalgia, announcing the addition of “Robotech” to its early 1998 lineup. This move aims to bolster an already robust roster, featuring the likes of "Voltron," "ThunderCats," "Cartoon Roulette," and "The Real Adventures of Jonny Quest," establishing Toonami as a bastion of classic and contemporary animation.

In a summer of animation debuts, July 14th marked the premiere of two of Cartoon Network's quirkiest series: "Johnny Bravo" and "Cow and Chicken." "Johnny Bravo," with its slick, Elvis-impersonating protagonist, dazzles with a unique blend of charm, wit, and undeniable narcissism. Meanwhile, "Cow and Chicken" indulge viewers’ appetites for the bizarre with off-the-wall humor that echoes the surreal tendencies of classic animation. Both shows demonstrate Cartoon Network's commitment to diverse and unconventional storytelling.

DIC Entertainment's "The Wacky World of Tex Avery" has been axed. Amidst critiques and ratings that failed to live up to expectations, the cancellation indicates that not all nostalgic revivals capture the magic of their predecessors.

In gaming news, the highly anticipated release of "Banjo-Kazooie" has been pushed back. The delay to June 1998 is attributed to the developers needing more time to polish this promising addition to the platformer genre, inciting fans to await its completion with bated breath.

And on the topic of gaming, the cultural phenomenon that is "Final Fantasy VII" continues to receive accolades for its storytelling, gameplay mechanics, and groundbreaking visual fidelity. Square's JRPG masterpiece has not only redefined the genre but has also established itself as an epochal title whose influence will be seen for years to come.

As the year progresses, it's evident that the worlds of animation, television, and gaming are in a constant state of evolution and innovation, inviting audiences to experience a broad spectrum of creative visions.

Title: Holiday Shopping Frenzy Nears as Black Friday Approaches; DiC Entertains Adult Animation Market; DreamWorks to Challenge Disney's Reign; Sega Gears Up 'Katana' Console; Gaming Giants Clash for Black Friday Dominance

Date: November 19, 1997

The holiday season is right around the corner, and consumers are gearing up for the annual retail extravaganza known as Black Friday. As families prepare to storm the aisles for the best deals and hottest gifts of the year, retailers are stocking up, predicting one of the biggest shopping days on record.

In the realm of entertainment, animation studio DiC is making waves with its strategy to tap into the adult animation market. Following the success stories like "South Park," DiC is rumored to be developing an "Einstone series," which promises to bring laughs to a more mature audience. This shift towards adult-oriented content signifies a notable pivot in DiC's traditional repertoire of child-friendly cartoons.

The cinematic landscape is bracing for a showdown as Disney comes face-to-face with the relative newcomer, DreamWorks. The rivalry is heating up as DreamWorks plans to release a slate of movies next year, including "ANTZ" and "Small Soldiers," potentially encroaching on Disney's turf. With both studios vying for market share, the stakes are high as each aims to outdo the other with their creative outputs.

Meanwhile, in the world of gaming, Sega is in preparation mode for its next venture into the console market with a device codenamed "Katana." As the industry eagerly awaits details, anticipation is high that Sega will deliver a platform to rival the successes of Sony's PlayStation and Nintendo's dominating N64.

Speaking of which, the PlayStation and Nintendo 64 are preparing for a face-off of their own this Black Friday. The consoles are going head-to-head with blockbuster titles like "Final Fantasy VII," "Mega Man X4," "Mario Kart 64," "Castlevania: Symphony of the Night," "Star Fox 64," and “Diddy Kong Racing.” Not to be outdone are "Crash Bandicoot 2: Cortex Strikes Back" and "Oddworld: Abe's Oddysee," which are also gearing up to be top sellers. These games represent the pinnacle of current gaming technology and storytelling, sure to be at the top of many holiday wish lists.

This Black Friday, whether it's the rush for the latest in animation, the battle of the big-screen heavyweights, or the quest for gaming supremacy, the excitement is palpable. The countdown has begun, and the holiday season is promising to bring fierce competition, fantastic entertainment, and, most important of all, joy to consumers everywhere.

Sega is take an sneak peek of the new sonic game in march of 1998.

Title: "StarCraft" Set for March Debut; Gaming Reviews: "Panzer Dragoon Saga" and "Resident Evil 2"; Fox Kids Rebrands to Fox Family; Sega Teases New Sonic; "Marvel vs. Capcom" Hits Arcades; ESRB Updates Rating System

Date: January 25, 1998

As the new year gains momentum, the video game industry is already showcasing signs of a monumental year. Blizzard Entertainment has announced that their highly anticipated real-time strategy game, "StarCraft," will be unleashing its interstellar warfare on Windows come March 31, 1998. Gamers are marking calendars for what is poised to be a defining moment for both the genre and Blizzard's illustrious reputation.

Rolling on to reviews in this thrilling gaming landscape, "Panzer Dragoon Saga" swoops in with an engrossing RPG experience for Sega Saturn enthusiasts. With mesmerizing graphics, a compelling storyline, and innovative gameplay mechanics, it's a title that looks set to ride high in the memories of Saturn fans as one of the console's defining epics.

Simultaneously, Capcom's "Resident Evil 2" has infected the Sony PlayStation with its release, garnering critical acclaim. This sequel to the survival horror classic amplifies the terror and expands the trail-blazing formula, cementing "Resident Evil 2" as a masterclass in suspenseful storytelling and atmospheric game design.

In television, Fox Kids Worldwide is evolving; by late summer, it will be known as Fox Family Worldwide, encompassing both Fox Family Channel's new look and style. This rebrand reflects a shift towards a broader demographic, embracing a mix of animated content and music to create a diverse entertainment portfolio for families worldwide.

Sega fans are all ears as the company announces a sneak peek of the next Sonic game, coming this March. Details remain under wraps, but the prospect of a new adventure with the iconic blue hedgehog is stirring excitement across the gaming community.

Over in the arcades, "Marvel vs. Capcom: Clash of Super Heroes" is punching its way into players' hearts with its frenetic crossover action. The title is a hit among arcade-goers, who revel in its exhilarating gameplay and the fantasy matchups between beloved characters from two entertainment powerhouses.

Finally, in the regulatory arena, the Entertainment Software Rating Board (ESRB) has revised its rating system. The "K-A" (Kids to Adults) rating is now updated to "E" (Everyone), a change designed to clarify the suitability of game content at a glance. This adjustment aligns with ESRB's commitment to providing guidance to parents and players in an evolving marketplace.

From the strategic depths of space to the tension-filled streets of Raccoon City, the gaming industry is setting a vigorous pace for 1998. As new titles gear up for release and industry giants redefine their brands, one thing is clear – this year promises to deliver relentless excitement and innovation for gamers worldwide.

Title: Cartoon Network Readies for "Powerpuff Girls" and "Ed, Edd n Eddy"; "CatDog" Pounces onto Nickelodeon; Fox Kids Gears Up for Fall; Mainframe Reflects on Success; Dive into Video Game Reviews

Date: February 27, 1998

This fall, Cartoon Network is manifesting its gusto by delivering back-to-back entertainment with the much-anticipated debuts of "The Powerpuff Girls" and "Ed, Edd n Eddy." The new additions come as dynamic contenders in the animated landscape, poised to capture the hearts and laughter of audiences in their living rooms. Meanwhile, the hit series "Johnny Bravo" is taking a breather amid news that its creator, Van Partible, has parted ways with Hanna-Barbera Cartoons.

Nickelodeon is not one to watch from the sidelines, as it prepares to unleash "CatDog" into its programming mix come April 4. The conjoined twins promise to serve a dose of comedic misadventures, already generating buzz among the channel's eager fans.

Over at Fox Kids, preparations for the 1998-1999 fall season are in full swing. Highlighting the lineup is "Power Rangers in Space," which has already rocketed in the ratings, alongside other promising titles such as "Mystic Knights," "Mad Jack the Pirate," "Oggy and the Cockroaches," "The Secret Files of the Spy Dogs," "The Mr. Potato Head Show," and "The New Woody Woodpecker Show." The network is pushing to keep the momentum going strong with these fresh additions.

In gaming news, reviews are in for "Burning Rangers," "Tenchu: Stealth Assassins," and "X-Men vs. Street Fighter: EX Edition," each game carving out its niche. "Burning Rangers" dazzles with fiery rescue missions, while "Tenchu" allows players to embrace their inner ninja with stealth-based gameplay. Meanwhile, "X-Men vs. Street Fighter" delivers explosive tag-team action to the PlayStation arena.


Adventure fans are treated to a lush experience with "Croc: Legend of the Gobbos," garnering praise for its charming lead character and vibrant world, despite notes regarding its challenging control scheme.

Then there's "Xenogears," Square's latest RPG epic that's stirring the pot with its in-depth narrative and hybrid turn-based mechanics. Players have been captivated by its philosophical overtones and engaging combat system, making "Xenogears" a standout example of storytelling finesse in the RPG genre.

Reflecting on their digital milestones, Mainframe Entertainment discusses their thoughts on "Beast Wars: Transformers" and "Reboot." Both shows have carved out a legacy in CGI animation, proving that innovative technology coupled with strong tales can define a generation of televised storytelling.

The industry is abuzz with these groundbreaking series and video game releases shaping the cultural tapestry. As networks and developers push to redefine genres and expectations alike, it's clear that in 1998, animations and gaming are on a trajectory to offer more content, imagination, and innovation than ever before.

a The Lost World: Jurassic Park review

Title: Leap Year Facts, Upcoming Film Excitement, and Warner Bros. Reevaluates Animated Features

Date: February 28, 1998

As February 29th returns to our calendars, marking the fascinating occurrence of a leap year, here are some leap year facts to ponder. Did you know that the leap year was introduced by Julius Caesar in 45 BCE in the Julian Calendar? And, astrologically speaking, it's technically not a leap year if it can be divided by 100, unless it can also be divided by 400. This peculiar adjustment keeps our calendars in correct alignment with Earth's revolutions around the Sun.

Amidst the trivia of leap year facts, Hollywood's animation and blockbuster scene is poised to ignite the summer box office. Disney fans eagerly await the release of "Mulan" on June 19, with enthusiasts ready to embrace the story of valor and honor that departs from traditional fairy tale narratives Disney is known for.

DreamWorks SKG is also charging into the fray with "Small Soldiers" set for a July 10 premiere. The film blends live-action with cutting-edge CGI effects, hoping to capture both young and mature audiences with its unique tale of toys coming to life.

Not to be outdone, TriStar's “Godzilla '98” is preparing to stomp into theaters on May 20, with marketers building anticipation around the new and secretive roar of the iconic monster. The film hopes to reboot the franchise for Western audiences and promises to be a spectacular cinematic event.

However, Warner Bros. will first deliver its own mythical adventure with "Quest for Camelot," launching on May 15. It will have only a week to win over audiences before "Godzilla '98" takes the spotlight. It's a bold scheduling move, given it precedes the monster movie craze by mere days.

Looking to the future, Warner Bros. Feature Animation is gearing up for "The Iron Giant" in 1999 after a challenging couple of years in the film arena. Between 1996 to 1998, plans for a plethora of films including "Sho and the Demons of the Deep," "WONKA," "The Sorcerer's Apprentice," and "Ray Gunn" have been abandoned. Additionally, the anticipated "Space Jam" sequel has slammed into a wall with Michael Jordan's refusal to star, forcing Warner Bros. to rethink its strategy for Feature Animation.

In the world of sequels, "The Lost World: Jurassic Park" seems to have managed to capture some of the magic that made its predecessor a global phenomenon. The review highlights the blockbuster's thrilling action sequences and impressive dinosaurs brought to life through a blend of animatronics and CG effects, even if the plot doesn’t always maintain the heights of the original.

As the calendar’s quirks give us reason to reflect, Hollywood marches forward with a parade of animated tales and cinematic giants vying to leave their mark on audiences worldwide. With exciting releases on the horizon and studios reassessing their animated projects, the film industry continues to evolve, promising thrills, heart, and spectacle in equal measure.

Title: The Digital Age Expands; "Blast Corps" Clears the Way; Sega's Dreamcast Unveiled; "Slayers" Charms Audiences; DiC and UPN Strike Deal; "StarCraft" Hype Builds; "The Amazing Spider-Man" #437 Swings High

Date: March 3, 1998

As the dawn of the 21st century approaches, the exponential expansion of the internet continues. Dubbed the "world wide web," this global network is revolutionizing communication, commerce, and entertainment, promising to connect humanity in ways previously unfathomable.

In the gaming world, Rare’s "Blast Corps" has made its explosive debut on the Nintendo 64. The concept is simple yet compelling: pilot a variety of vehicles and destroy buildings to clear a path for an out-of-control nuclear missile carrier. Reviews praise its originality, tight controls, and addictively destructive gameplay, making "Blast Corps" an instant classic in the system's library.

Sega is set to raise the curtains on their latest console endeavor, the Dreamcast, later this month. Named to evoke the infinite possibilities of a player's dreams, the console is scheduled for a late 1998 launch in Japan, with a North American release to follow in 1999. Gamers worldwide are on the edge of their seats, eager for a glimpse of the future of gaming.

Anime fans rejoice as reviews for "Slayers" and the "Slayers the Movie: Perfect Edition" are in. The series, alongside its larger-than-life cinematic version, delivers a perfect blend of fantasy, comedy, and magic, with fans celebrating the brilliant English adaptation and the delightful escapades of its fiery protagonist Lina Inverse.

In television, DiC Entertainment has inked a lucrative deal with the UPN network to bring adult animation to a wider audience. This partnership underlines the growing trend of animated content breaking from its traditional youth demographic, catering to a mature audience that has shown a keen interest in animated storytelling.

Ahead of its March 31st release, "StarCraft" facts are coming into the limelight, with enthusiasts tracing its history. Developed by Blizzard Entertainment, "StarCraft" is setting out to redefine real-time strategy games with its intricate balance, deep lore, and competitive edge. Anticipation for the game's strategic space opera has reached fever pitch, with its beta testing heralding unanimous excitement.

On the pages of Marvel Comics, "The Amazing Spider-Man" #437 swings into comic book stores. With its rich narrative and dynamic artwork, the issue continues to reveal the web-slinger's strength in character development and action. Comic book aficionados and causal readers alike marvel at Spider-Man’s unwavering appeal, pitting him against adversaries that challenge both his physical prowess and moral fortitude.

In an age where digital technologies, gaming, animation, and storytelling interweave more seamlessly than ever, the excitement for new releases and advances in all facets of entertainment is both palpable and infectious. Be it through consoles or comic panels, the art of engaging audiences continues to evolve, lending a fresh and invigorating pulse to popular culture.

Title: "Sonic Adventure" Set for December Debut in Japan; Sega Puts Faith in Dreamcast; DiC Reflects on "Tex Avery" Missteps; Seth MacFarlane Crafts "Family Guy"; "Banjo-Kazooie" Update; "South Park" Movie Planned; Exploring "Spawn"

Date: March 11, 1998

The gaming community buzzes with anticipation as Sega announces the official release date for "Sonic Adventure." This groundbreaking title for the Dreamcast is scheduled to spin dash onto the Japanese market on December 23, with a North American release slated for the following year. As the sky-blue hedgehog embraces a bold 3D world, gamers eagerly await what is heralded as Sonic's most thrilling outing yet.

The weight of expectation presses on Sega's shoulders, with the company banking on the Dreamcast to succeed in Japan—especially in light of the Saturn's less-than-stellar performance in the U.S. market. Sega hopes that Dreamcast, with its advanced graphics and innovative features, will restore its luster in the console race and avoid the pitfalls that left the Saturn behind its rivals.

Television animation studio DiC has candidly spoken to viewers about the shortcomings of "The Wacky World of Tex Avery." The series, intended as a homage to the legendary animator, fell short of that mark. In an attempt to recapture viewer interest, DiC proposes a pivot towards adult animation, hinting at a series reminiscent of the charm of "The Flintstones" and the wit of "The Simpsons."

In the bustling world of animated television, Seth MacFarlane is busy at work on a pilot named "Family Guy" for Fox. Having departed his position at Hanna-Barbera, MacFarlane's new venture is expected to bring a fresh, comedic voice to prime-time television, with a particular focus on delivering satirical and culturally reflective humor.

Video game developer Rareware addresses its ambitious project "Banjo-Kazooie," announcing an official release date of June 29, 1998. The extra time invested is anticipated to perfect this vibrant 3D platformer, with the potential to become one of the Nintendo 64's standout experiences.

On the film front, the irreverent humor of "South Park" is set to grace the big screen. Creators Trey Parker and Matt Stone have indicated a theatrical feature is in development, aiming for a summer 1999 release. Fans of the small-town Colorado kids are eager for their larger-than-life cinematic escapade, which is expected to push boundaries even further than the TV series.

Exploring the darker recesses of comic book lore, "Spawn" continues to fascinate readers with its brooding narrative and complex antihero, Al Simmons. Spawning a cult following since its inception, Todd McFarlane's creation has redefined the antihero archetype while navigating themes of morality and redemption, further immortalized by the 1997 feature film and animated series on HBO.

As diverse forms of entertainment converge, the lines between television, gaming, and comics blur, offering avenues for storytelling that challenge conventions and enthrall audiences. Whether through the capture-the-flag antics in a virtual Station Square or the satirical layers of Quahog, Rhode Island, the shaping of contemporary pop culture through these mediums is unmistakable and enduring.

The Prince of Egypt is a 1998 American animated musical drama film produced by DreamWorks Animation and released by DreamWorks Pictures. The first feature film from DreamWorks to be traditionally animated and their second overall, it is an adaptation of the first fourteen chapters of the Book of Exodus and follows the life of Moses from being a prince of Egypt to a prophet of the Lord, chosen by God to carry out his ultimate destiny of leading the Hebrews out of Egypt. The film was directed by Brenda Chapman, Steve Hickner, and Simon Wells (in Chapman and Hickner's feature directorial debuts) with a screenplay written by Philip LaZebnik. It features songs written by Stephen Schwartz and a score composed by Hans Zimmer. The voice cast consists of Val Kilmer, Ralph Fiennes, Michelle Pfeiffer, Sandra Bullock, Jeff Goldblum, Danny Glover, Patrick Stewart, Helen Mirren, Steve Martin, and Martin Short. and set to December 18, 1998.
Antz is a 1998 American animated adventure comedy film produced by DreamWorks Animation (in its debut film) and Pacific Data Images and released by DreamWorks Pictures. It was directed by Eric Darnell and Tim Johnson (in their feature directorial debuts) from a screenplay by Todd Alcott, Chris Weitz, and Paul Weitz. The film features the voices of Woody Allen, Sharon Stone, Jennifer Lopez, Sylvester Stallone, Christopher Walken, Dan Aykroyd, Anne Bancroft, Danny Glover and Gene Hackman. Some of the main characters share facial similarities with the actors who voice them.[10] The film involves an anxious worker ant, Z (Allen), who falls in love with Princess Bala (Stone). When the treacherous scheming of the arrogant officer General Mandible (Hackman) threatens to wipe out the entire worker population, Z must save the ant colony from the flooded tunnel and strives to make social inroads. released on October 2, 1998
Aardman Animations is making it's first movie chicken run to set go by dreamworks in 2000 anlong with The Road to El Dorado.

Title: DreamWorks Shuffles Slate: Cancellations and New Directions; "Prince of Egypt," "Antz" on Horizons; Aardman Partnership Takes Flight

Date: March 13, 1998

In a surprising development, DreamWorks SKG has reshuffled its animation deck, canceling several of its film projects, including "Rockumentary" and "The Wanderer," along with the Jurassic Park-themed "Chaos Effect." These cancellations signal a strategic pivot for the fledgling animation studio as it carves out its identity in a competitive market.

Despite the cuts, DreamWorks is moving full steam ahead with "Tusker," an ambitious project in their lineup. However, release plans for this tale remain hazy, reflecting the uncertain nature of pioneering new animated stories in today's saturated entertainment landscape.

Two animated features stand poised to bolster DreamWorks' profile in the near future. "The Prince of Egypt," directed by Brenda Chapman, Steve Hickner, and Simon Wells, is an adaptation of the biblical story of Moses. Heralded as DreamWorks Animation's first traditionally animated film, slated for a December 18, 1998 release, it boasts an impressive voice cast, including Val Kilmer and Ralph Fiennes, and music by Stephen Schwartz and Hans Zimmer, promising an epic narrative rich in drama and spritual depth.

Adding to their foray into animation, DreamWorks will also unveil "Antz" on October 2, 1998. This animated comedy, directed by Eric Darnell and Tim Johnson, brings a stellar ensemble voice cast, headlined by Woody Allen and Sharon Stone, to the story of a neurotic worker ant striving against a rigid class system—a narrative sure to invite comparisons to the social analogies in Disney's "A Bug's Life."

In a partnership extending across the pond, DreamWorks has joined forces with Aardman Animations, best known for their "Wallace & Gromit" claymation series. Their collaboration will first materialize with "Chicken Run," aiming for a 2000 release and followed by "The Road to El Dorado," as DreamWorks continues to diversify its portfolio with distinctive animated visions.

The industry is taking note as DreamWorks and Disney engage in an increasingly public battle for animation dominance. The former has aimed to differentiate itself with bold narratives and attention to detail. The latter, a storied pioneer with an established kingdom of classics, sees its newest competitor as both a challenger and a catalyst to its own evolution.

As "The Prince of Egypt" and "Antz" prepare to enter the fray alongside anticipated Disney releases, the stage is set for a cinematic showdown that may well redefine the power dynamics of animated filmmaking. In the end, it may not be a question of who emerges as the victor, but how the art of animation will flourish through the rivalry, offering audiences the true spoils of this creative competition.
